answer:
In *Andreas*, the sea becomes a perilous adversary during St. Andrew's voyage to rescue Matthew, whipping up a divinely orchestrated storm that tests the faith and resolve of Andrew and his companions.[^1] The waters churn violently—"the whale-mere was stirred... winds waxed wild, waves ground together"—filling the thanes with terror as "sea-dread stood in threatening throngs," leading them to doubt survival.[^2] Yet Andrew remains steadfast, praising God amid the tumult, embodying heroic Christian endurance; the sea separates him momentarily from divine reassurance (as the disguised Christ calms it indirectly) but ultimately carries him forward under God's providence.

> Then the deep was disturbed, the whale-mere was stirred. The horn-fish played, gliding through the spear-sea, and the grey gull, slaughter-greedy, circled. The sky-candle darkened, winds waxed wild, waves ground together, streams were stirred up, the stays sang out, the sails were soaked. The sea-dread stood in threatening throngs. The thanes became fearful in spirit.[^1]

In *Elene*, by contrast, the sea figures more statically as a created expanse within God's ordered cosmos, invoked in prayer rather than as an active peril; it "carries" messengers safely between realms without testing St. Helena (Elene) or Judas directly.[^3] Judas praises the deity who wrought "heaven and earth and the ocean’s tumult, the sea’s wide bosom," beseeching a miracle on land (revealing the True Cross); later, Elene's envoys traverse the "high sea" and "swan-road" unhindered, reuniting her with Constantine. Here, the sea symbolizes vast divine dominion, separating the queen from her emperor yet bridged effortlessly by faith-guided voyages—no storm, no trial for the saint.

> “Lord Savior... you who wrought through your glory’s might heaven and earth and the ocean’s tumult, the sea’s wide bosom, and all creation together...”[^3]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-8fd929).
[^2]: Ibid., [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-258366).
[^3]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, ch. 1, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1).
